_schema: Historical revolution benchmarks mapped to Revolution Index factors
_note: Factor values are scholarly estimates on the same 0.0-1.0 scale as current.json factors. For institutional_trust, lower values = less trust = worse for stability. Composite estimates are approximate overall severity scores (0-100).

benchmarks.name: French Revolution
benchmarks.narrative: The French monarchy collapsed under extreme wealth concentration, crushing tax burdens on commoners, and a bankrupt state unable to feed its people. The top 10% held 90% of national wealth while bread consumed 50-80% of a laborer's wages. The storming of the Bastille became the defining symbol of popular revolt against institutional failure.

economic_inequality.note: Top 10% held 90% of wealth; income Gini ~0.59; wealth Gini ~0.80-0.85

political_polarization.note: Three Estates system; aristocracy, clergy, and commoners in open class conflict

protest_intensity.note: Bread riots across France; storming of the Bastille; mass mobilization of sans-culottes

institutional_trust.note: Monarchy, Catholic Church, and tax system all despised; state functionally bankrupt

unemployment_stress.note: State bankrupt; bread consumed 50-80% of wages; widespread food insecurity

sources: Morrisson & Snyder, 'The Income Inequality of France in Historical Perspective' (2000)
Thomas Piketty, 'Capital in the Twenty-First Century' (2014)
CEPR: Economic Consequences of the French Revolution

benchmarks.name: Revolutions of 1848
benchmarks.narrative: The most widespread revolutionary wave in European history swept across 50+ countries in a single year, triggered by catastrophic crop failures and financial crisis. Potato blight devastated Northern Europe, German rye harvests fell to 20% of normal, and wheat prices doubled across France and Italy. In Vienna alone, 10,000 factory workers lost their jobs.

economic_inequality.note: Moderate but growing; aristocratic land ownership vs urban working class

political_polarization.note: Autocratic monarchies vs liberal/nationalist reform movements across continent

protest_intensity.note: 50+ countries simultaneously; the most contagious revolutionary wave in history

institutional_trust.note: Widespread distrust of autocratic governments; demands for constitutional reform

unemployment_stress.note: Crop failures 1845-47; food prices doubled; mass factory layoffs; workers spent 50%+ on food

sources: Berger & Spoerer, 'Economic Crises and the European Revolutions of 1848'
Khan Academy, 'Revolutions 1750-1900'
Encyclopedia.com, 'Revolutions of 1848'

benchmarks.name: Mexican Revolution
benchmarks.narrative: After 35 years of authoritarian rule under Porfirio Diaz, Mexico erupted in revolution. A tiny elite owned vast estates while peasants were dispossessed of communal lands. Sugar workers were employed only 4 months per year, living in perpetual debt. Widespread malnutrition and illiteracy plagued rural areas that lacked any healthcare or education.

economic_inequality.note: Vast estates owned by tiny elite; communal ejido lands privatized; extreme land concentration

political_polarization.note: Diaz dictatorship vs peasants, workers, intellectuals, and competing revolutionary factions

protest_intensity.note: Student riots and demonstrations growing before armed revolt; rural uprisings

institutional_trust.note: Porfirian dictatorship seen as corrupt and serving only the elite

unemployment_stress.note: Seasonal employment (4 months/year); perpetual debt; malnutrition; rural destitution

benchmarks.keystat: Sugar workers employed only 4 months per year
sources: Cambridge Core, 'Income Inequality in Mexico 1895-1940'
Britannica, 'Mexican Revolution'
Cal State Journals, 'The Mexican Revolution: An Economic and Social Revival'

benchmarks.name: Russian Revolution
benchmarks.narrative: World War I devastated an already fragile Russian economy. Prices quadrupled between 1914-1917 while bread shortages triggered mass protests in Petrograd. A tiny nobility and urban elite controlled virtually all wealth while millions of peasants endured periodic famines with insecure land tenure. Tsar Nicholas II was seen as catastrophically incompetent.

economic_inequality.note: Tiny nobility/elite vs millions of impoverished peasants and workers; extreme wealth gap

political_polarization.note: Bolsheviks, Mensheviks, Social Revolutionaries, monarchists — multiple armed factions

protest_intensity.note: Petrograd bread riots; general strikes; soldiers refusing orders; armed insurrection

institutional_trust.note: Tsar seen as incompetent; military failures; government unable to provide basic food

unemployment_stress.note: Wartime inflation (prices 4x in 3 years); bread shortages; mass poverty; WWI devastation

sources: NBER, 'Russian Inequality on the Eve of Revolution' (Nafziger & Lindert, 2013)
CEPR, 'Russia's National Income in War and Revolution 1913-1928'
History.com, 'Russian Revolution'

benchmarks.name: Iranian Revolution
benchmarks.narrative: Iran's economy was growing on paper, but oil boom profits flowed to a small elite connected to the Shah while rapid urbanization created a massive disenfranchised underclass. Two-thirds of the population was under 30, and young people faced unemployment despite modernization. The Shah was seen as a Western puppet enforced by the feared SAVAK secret police.

economic_inequality.note: Oil wealth concentrated in elite; urban underclass created by rapid modernization

political_polarization.note: Secular modernizers vs religious conservatives vs leftists; all united against Shah

protest_intensity.note: Millions marched; strikes paralyzed the economy; sustained mass mobilization

institutional_trust.note: Shah seen as Western puppet; SAVAK secret police; autocracy despite modernization

unemployment_stress.note: High inflation despite GDP growth; youth unemployment from modernization disruption

benchmarks.keystat: Two-thirds of the population was under age 30
sources: Britannica, 'Iranian Revolution'
Stanford, 'Iranian Revolution of 1979'
History.com, 'What Led to the 1979 Iranian Revolution'

benchmarks.name: Arab Spring
benchmarks.narrative: Beginning with a fruit vendor's self-immolation in Tunisia, uprisings swept across the Arab world fueled by extreme youth unemployment (30% in Tunisia, 25% in Egypt), decades of corrupt autocracy, and global food price spikes. Social media amplified grievances and enabled rapid mobilization. Youth unemployment across the region was the highest in the world.

economic_inequality.note: Official Gini 30-40% but scholars say surveys dramatically undercount elite wealth

political_polarization.note: Autocratic regimes vs popular movements; less factional than other revolutions

protest_intensity.note: Millions across multiple countries; social media amplification; regimes toppled

institutional_trust.note: Decades of corrupt autocracy; rigged elections; suppressed civil society

unemployment_stress.note: Tunisia 30% youth unemployment; Egypt 25%; global food price spike 2010-2011

benchmarks.keystat: Youth unemployment reached 30% in Tunisia and 25% in Egypt
sources: Al Jazeera, 'How Economic Hardship Fuelled the Arab Spring' (2020)
Wilson Center, 'Forecasting Instability: The Case of the Arab Spring'
Brookings, 'Youth Employment in Egypt and Tunisia'

benchmarks.name: Sri Lanka Crisis
benchmarks.narrative: Sri Lanka's government defaulted on its debt after years of fiscal mismanagement, populist tax cuts, and the COVID-19 tourism collapse. Inflation hit 54.6% overall and 81% for food. Citizens stormed the presidential palace, and the president fled the country. It demonstrated how pure economic collapse — without pre-existing ideological movements — can topple a government.

economic_inequality.note: Present but not the primary driver; crisis was about total economic collapse

political_polarization.note: Population was relatively united against the government rather than factionally divided

protest_intensity.note: Protesters stormed and occupied the presidential palace; president fled country

institutional_trust.note: Government seen as fiscally irresponsible; ruling family dynasty despised

unemployment_stress.note: 54.6% inflation; 81% food inflation; sovereign debt default; foreign reserves depleted

benchmarks.keystat: Food inflation reached 81% — the highest in South Asian history
sources: Wikipedia, 'Sri Lankan Economic Crisis (2019-2024)'
World Economic Forum, 'Visual Breakdown of Sri Lanka Crisis'
IMF, 'Sri Lanka Economic Reform Program'

benchmarks.name: Nepal Gen Z Protests
benchmarks.narrative: Nepal's Gen Z organized through Discord to topple a government seen as corrupt and out of touch. With 20.8% youth unemployment, an average income of just $1,400/year, and 33% of GDP dependent on remittances, frustration boiled over when ruling elites displayed lavish wealth on social media. The movement chose its interim leader through online polling — a first in history.

economic_inequality.note: Elite wealth visible on social media vs $1,400/year average income; 33% GDP from remittances

political_polarization.note: Primarily generational divide (Gen Z vs establishment) rather than ideological factions

protest_intensity.note: Nationwide Discord-organized protests; government toppled; digital-first mobilization

institutional_trust.note: Government seen as corrupt; social media exposed elite wealth vs mass poverty

unemployment_stress.note: 20.8% youth unemployment; $1,400/yr average income; remittance-dependent economy

benchmarks.keystat: 20.8% youth unemployment; interim leader chosen via online poll
sources: Wikipedia, '2025 Nepalese Gen Z Protests'
Columbia Journalism Review, 'Nepal's Revolution Was on Discord'
The Nation, 'Nepal's Revolution Wasn't Televised'
